contents.name: CON.txt
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension — Windows strips the extension before checking reservations, so CON.txt is just as reserved as CON

contents.name: CON.bat
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with .bat extension

contents.name: CON.log
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with .log extension

contents.name: PRN.log
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension

contents.name: PRN.bat
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension

contents.name: NUL.txt
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension

contents.name: NUL.dat
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension

contents.name: AUX.cfg
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with extension

contents.name: COM1.txt
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved serial port with extension

contents.name: COM1.prn
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved serial port with extension

contents.name: COM9.txt
contents.invalid_reason: Upper-boundary COM9 with extension — COM1-COM9 are all reserved

contents.name: LPT1.prn
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved parallel port with extension

contents.name: LPT1.log
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved parallel port with extension

contents.name: LPT9.log
contents.invalid_reason: Upper-boundary LPT9 with extension — LPT1-LPT9 are all reserved

contents.name: CON.
contents.invalid_reason: Double-strip trap — Windows strips the trailing dot first, then the reservation check fires on the bare CON

contents.name: NUL.
contents.invalid_reason: Double-strip trap — NUL. variant

contents.name: PRN.
contents.invalid_reason: Double-strip trap — PRN. variant

contents.name: CON
contents.invalid_reason: Double-strip trap — trailing space stripped, then reserved check

contents.name: CON . . .
contents.invalid_reason: Multi-strip trap — all trailing dots and spaces stripped, leaves reserved CON

contents.name: document.txt:hidden_stream
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS Alternate Data Stream — the base filename is document.txt, with a secondary data stream named hidden_stream. Many extraction tools lose or mis-handle the stream portion

contents.name: image.jpg:Zone.Identifier
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS ADS for Mark-of-the-Web — the stream Windows attaches to downloaded files to record their origin. Tools that preserve ADS must preserve this; tools that strip it silently lose security metadata

contents.name: file.txt::$DATA
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS ADS explicit $DATA type — file.txt::$DATA is equivalent to file.txt on NTFS. Archive tools that don't canonicalise stream names can create duplicate-looking entries

contents.name: CON:stream
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device name with ADS suffix — compounds the reserved-name check with the stream-split parser

contents.name: file.txt:ads:$DATA
contents.invalid_reason: ADS with explicit stream type specifier — parsers that split only on the first colon vs the last get different filenames

contents.name: :anonymous_stream
contents.invalid_reason: Filename starting with colon — tools that interpret leading colons as empty-base ADS references produce a zero-length base filename

contents.name: $MFT
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS Master File Table metafile — reserved at the volume level. An archive extracted to an NTFS root containing this name can confuse volume tools

contents.name: $MFTMirr
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS MFT mirror metafile

contents.name: $LogFile
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS transaction log metafile

contents.name: $Volume
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS volume descriptor metafile

contents.name: $AttrDef
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS attribute definition metafile

contents.name: $Bitmap
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS allocation bitmap metafile

contents.name: $Boot
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS boot sector metafile

contents.name: $BadClus
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS bad cluster list metafile

contents.name: $Secure
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS security descriptor metafile

contents.name: $UpCase
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS uppercase conversion table metafile

contents.name: $Extend
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS extended metafile directory

contents.name: $Quota
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS quota metafile (inside $Extend)

contents.name: $ObjId
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS object ID metafile (inside $Extend)

contents.name: $Reparse
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS reparse point metafile (inside $Extend)

contents.name: $UsnJrnl
contents.invalid_reason: NTFS USN change journal metafile

contents.name: control__SOH
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+0001 (Start of Heading) — forbidden in NTFS filenames (0x01–0x1F all forbidden)

contents.name: control_	_HT
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+0009 (Horizontal Tab) — forbidden in NTFS filenames

contents.name:
control_
_LF
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+000A (Line Feed) — forbidden in NTFS, also breaks tar output line-parsing

contents.name: control__CR
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+000D (Carriage Return) — forbidden in NTFS

contents.name: control__ESC
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+001B (Escape) — forbidden in NTFS, enables terminal-escape injection if printed

contents.name: control__US
contents.invalid_reason: Control character U+001F (Unit Separator, upper boundary of 0x00–0x1F forbidden range)

contents.name: boot.ini
contents.invalid_reason: Legacy Windows boot loader configuration file — Windows-protected in boot contexts

contents.name: config.sys
contents.invalid_reason: DOS/Windows system configuration file — legacy system-reserved

contents.name: autoexec.bat
contents.invalid_reason: DOS/Windows autoexec batch file — legacy system-reserved

contents.name: ntldr
contents.invalid_reason: Windows NT boot loader — system-reserved

contents.name: ntdetect.com
contents.invalid_reason: Windows NT hardware detection binary — system-reserved

contents.name: msdos.sys
contents.invalid_reason: Legacy MS-DOS system file

contents.name: io.sys
contents.invalid_reason: Legacy MS-DOS system file

contents.name: command.com
contents.invalid_reason: Legacy MS-DOS command interpreter

contents.name: bootmgr.efi
contents.invalid_reason: UEFI boot manager binary — the modern counterpart to the bootmgr entry in bad_windows.json

contents.name: Autorun.inf
contents.invalid_reason: Windows autorun descriptor — historically exploited for drive-by execution on removable media

contents.name: autorun.inf
contents.invalid_reason: Autorun.inf lowercase variant — NTFS case-preserves but Windows is case-insensitive, so both resolve to the same file

contents.name: desktop.ini
contents.invalid_reason: Windows folder-customisation descriptor — legal but special-cased by Explorer

contents.name: ntuser.dat
contents.invalid_reason: User registry hive — locked by Windows when user is logged in

contents.name: ntuser.dat.LOG
contents.invalid_reason: User registry hive transaction log

contents.name: ntuser.dat.LOG1
contents.invalid_reason: User registry hive transaction log variant

contents.name: PROGRA~1
contents.invalid_reason: 8.3 short name for Program Files — Windows auto-generates this; an archive containing this literal name collides with the kernel-generated short name

contents.name: DOCUME~1
contents.invalid_reason: 8.3 short name — Windows auto-generated this for the legacy "Documents and Settings" directory

contents.name: MYLONG~1
contents.invalid_reason: 8.3 short name pattern — the ~N suffix is how NTFS disambiguates collisions

contents.name: TEST~123
contents.invalid_reason: 8.3 short name pattern with multi-digit disambiguation

contents.name: \\?\LongPath
contents.invalid_reason: UNC path prefix \\?\ — Windows uses this to opt into the 32K-character path limit. A filename starting with this literal is legal on POSIX but tools that parse it as a UNC prefix get confused

contents.name: \\.\PhysicalDrive0
contents.invalid_reason: Device namespace prefix \\.\ — Windows uses this to access raw devices

contents.name: \\server\share
contents.invalid_reason: UNC network path pattern — Windows tools attempt to resolve as a share

contents.name: \\LOCALHOST\C$
contents.invalid_reason: UNC admin share pattern — legitimate on Windows, problematic as a literal filename

contents.name: %WINDIR%
contents.invalid_reason: Environment variable lookalike — tools that %-expand filenames will substitute the Windows directory path

contents.name: %USERPROFILE%
contents.invalid_reason: Environment variable lookalike — expands to the user's profile directory

contents.name: %ProgramFiles%
contents.invalid_reason: Environment variable lookalike — expands to the program files directory

contents.name: %SystemRoot%
contents.invalid_reason: Environment variable lookalike

contents.name: file&calc.exe
contents.invalid_reason: Batch-file command separator & in filename — cmd.exe interprets this as a command chain

contents.name: file^notepad
contents.invalid_reason: Batch-file escape character ^ in filename — cmd.exe uses ^ as line continuation and escape

contents.name: file;calc
contents.invalid_reason: PowerShell / batch command separator in filename

contents.name: C:
contents.invalid_reason: Drive letter reference as filename — Windows treats C: as the current directory on drive C, not a file named C:

contents.name: D:
contents.invalid_reason: Drive letter reference

contents.name: A:
contents.invalid_reason: Drive letter reference — historically the floppy drive

contents.name: COM:
contents.invalid_reason: Reserved device prefix with trailing colon — parses as a drive-letter reference to a device rather than a filename
All-dots-and-spaces name — Windows strips trailing dots and spaces; this name strips to empty and gets rejected. Infinite-loop candidate in path-normalising code
All-spaces name (longer than the 3-space variant in bad_windows.json) — Windows strips all of them, leaving empty
